2: <header>1. Introduction </header>

Livestock production has increased and intensified as a result of higher food demand, due to the rapidly increasing world pop- ulation, with limited farming land (Ilea, 2009; Rosegrant et al., 2001). Pork represented 37% of the global meat production in 2007 (FAOSTAT, 2009). In 2007, global pig manure generation was estimated to be approximately 125 million tonnes of dry matter (Prapaspongsa et al., 2010). With the large generated amount and farming intensification, improper manure management has contributed to significant environmental problems. According to Lopez-Riduara et al. (2009) and Sandars et al. (2003), the main concerns were global warming from greenhouse gas (GHG)
emissions, aquatic eutrophication and acidification from ammonia emissions.

However, recently studies have indicated that ammonia emis- sions can potentially contribute to other types of impacts, which have not been included before (Anderson et al., 2002; Petersen et al., 2007). This includes respiratory effects on human from inorganic substances (respiratory inorganics) and terrestrial eutrophication - both potentially caused by ammonia emission from pig manure (Anderson et al., 2002; Petersen et al., 2007). Ammonia is a well-known precursor of particulate matter formu- lation and can form secondary particulate matters such as ammo- nium sulphate or ammonium nitrate - contributing to respiratory effects (Anderson et al., 2002). In the case of eutrophication, terrestrial eutrophication refers to impacts on native vegetation, forests and agricultural crops. Since nitrogen (N) is often a limiting nutrient, an additional N deposit (through ammonia and N-related emissions) on soils or vegetation can affect the terrestrial ecosys- items. Increased N deposits may favour some species over others and hence may influence the biodiversity (Bouwman et al., 2002;
Krupa, 2003). The evidence of these impacts confirms the need to include the additional impacts in environmental assessments of piggery waste management.

To reduce the environmental impacts, many technologies have been developed and applied to decrease the emissions of greenhouse gases and ammonia, and to recover energy and nutrients. Different treatment technologies (e.g. anaerobic digestion) applied to pig manure management can reduce and save greenhouse gas emissions from reduced methane (CH4) emissions and displaced electricity and heat production (Prapaspongsa et al., 2010; Petersen et al., 2007). Different manure storage systems are able to reduce ammonia emissions from 2% to 70% of nitrogen (N) content from pig manure (Hutchings et al., 2001; Rotz, 2004). Furthermore, spreading tech- nologies at the land application stage have contributed to the reduction of ammonia emissions. For example, injection systems may reduce ammonia emissions by more than 70% compared with a broadcast spreading system (Rotz, 2004). Nonetheless, the reduc- tion of ammonia emissions can induce N emissions in other forms, such as N20 or nitrate (NO), especially in the case of high unavail- able N contents remaining in the pig manure and the subsequent N surplus in the soil after spreading the manure on the land.

Despite the existing research into technological improvements, the focus has been relatively narrow and only encompassed one or a few handling stages such as treatment and land application stages (Lopez-Ridaura et al., 2009; Sorensen et al., 2003). Also some studies did not consider manure treatment at all, or considered only a single unit process (e.g. either composting or anaerobic digestion) (Basset- Mens and van der Werf, 2005; Van der Werf et al., 2007). Although whole farm perspective assessments have been performed, the results have often disregarded the specific details on mass and energy flows of pig manure management systems (Petersen et al., 2007). Hence, there is a need for a detailed assessment of overall environmental impacts from pig manure management incorpo- rating available technologies applied at different handling stages in order to reduce the environmental burdens.

6: In order to reduce the environmental impacts effectively, the objectives of this study are 1) to identify the most significant envi- ronmental impacts arising from pig manure management taking into consideration of a wide span of different impact categories and 2) to determine which technology system at which handling stage can significantly minimise the impacts. With respect to facilitating future improvements, it is important to be site specific and this study is based on European practices. Specifically, Danish conditions for electricity and heat systems are considered. To take the wide range of environmental impacts into consideration, life cycle assessment (LCA) is applied as a tool for the impact quantification.
7: The functional unit applied in this assessment is the treatment and disposal of 1 ton of raw pig manure (wet weight). This investi- gation considers pig manure management from cradle to grave. The cradle is defined as when manure leaves the pig barns. The grave is defined as when pig manure residuals are transformed into emis- sions (to air, water and soils) leaving system boundary (see Fig. 1).
8: The general framework for LCA is described in the ISO 14040 and 14044 standards which are basically followed in this study (ISO 14040, 2006; ISO 14044, 2006).

9: <header>2.1. Pig manure management system and scope definition </header>

The included life cycle stages are treatment, storage, trans- portation and land application as presented in Fig. 1. The modelled changes consider the treatment, storage and land application stages (Fig. 1). Since the handling stages prior to the treatment stage, such as housing, are not affected by the considered changes, they are excluded from this assessment. This is in accor- dance with the requirement from the ISO 14044 standard (ISO 14044, 2006). The considered technologies at different handling stages are listed in Table 1. The reference systems at different stages are chosen, with respect to wide application in many countries and named the TRE1, STO1 and LA1 systems (Table 1). At the treatment stage, the four systems (TRE1 to 4) are selected from the most promising and traditional integrated treatment scenarios according to Prapaspongsa et al. (2010), where the systems are described in detail. At the storage stage, the chosen reference system (STO1; anaerobic lagoon without a concrete floor) is commonly applied in many countries in Europe, the United States and Canada. The alternative system (STO2; storage tank with a natural crust) has also been extensively used in Denmark (Rotz, 2004; Petersen and Miller, 2006). For STO2, when the slurry is stored in a tank without mechanical agitation, the crust of organic
Table 1 Process description of technology systems at different stages.

System|Process Description |At the treatment stage: integrated treatment systems TRE1|No treatment (reference system) TRE2|Anaerobic digestion TRE3|Solid/liquid separation to 26% dry matter-drying-incineration TRE4|Solid/liquid separation to 26% dry matter-drying-thermal, gasification |At the storage stage: liquid storage systems STO1|Anaerobic lagoon without concrete floor (reference system) STO2|Storage tank with natural crust |At land application stage: liquid spreading systems LA1|Surface application: broadcast spreading/splash plate (reference system) LA2|Surface application: band spreading/trailing hoses LA3|Fast incorporation (within LA4|Shallow injection: open slot LA5|Deep injection: closed slot
Treatment system (TRE), storage system (STO), land application system (LA).

fibres can be formed naturally if the dry matter content of the slurry is high enough (Petersen et al., 2005). At the transport stage, the considered distance has two ranges: short (4.5 km) and long km) (assumptions based on Seadi, 2000) to represent the practical condition in Denmark which has environmental restric- tions on land application. In the systems without solid/liquid separation (incorporated in TRE1 and 2, see Table 1), all the liquids are transported long distance. In the systems including solid/liquid separation units (incorporated in TRE3 and 4, see Table 1), the liquids are transported short distance and the solids incorporated with the long distance transport. At the land application stage, there are five spreading techniques considered. The broadcast spreading system (LA1), where manure is spread to the soil surface through a splash plate, is widely used for manure application (McLaughlin et al., 2006; Sogaard et al., 2002). Other chosen systems (LA2 to 5) are the methods that have been used for the reduction of ammonia emissions (Rotz, 2004). The band spreading system (LA2) applies manure close to the soil surface in narrow bands via a number of hoses (Misselbrook et al., 2002). Rapid incorporation (LA3) is the method that incorporates the manure into the topsoil (e.g. via cultivators with rigid tines, spring tines, disc, etc.) after surface spreading within 6 h (Rotz, 2004; Huijsmans et al., 2003). Additionally, injection methods that inject manure under the ground via open slots up to 50 mm deep (shallow injection, LA4) and via closed slots more than 150 mm (deep injection, LA5) are considered (Misselbrook et al., 2002).

In the systems including solid/liquid separation units (incorporated in TRE3 and 4, see Table 1), the liquids are transported short distance and the solids incorporated with the long distance transport. At the land application stage, there are five spreading techniques considered. The broadcast spreading system (LA1), where manure is spread to the soil surface through a splash plate, is widely used for manure application (McLaughlin et al., 2006; Sogaard et al., 2002). Other chosen systems (LA2 to 5) are the methods that have been used for the reduction of ammonia emissions (Rotz, 2004). The band spreading system (LA2) applies manure close to the soil surface in narrow bands via a number of hoses (Misselbrook et al., 2002). Rapid incorporation (LA3) is the method that incorporates the manure into the topsoil (e.g. via cultivators with rigid tines, spring tines, disc, etc.) after surface spreading within 6 h (Rotz, 2004; Huijsmans et al., 2003). Additionally, injection methods that inject manure under the ground via open slots up to 50 mm deep (shallow injection, LA4) and via closed slots more than 150 mm (deep injection, LA5) are considered (Misselbrook et al., 2002).
12: <header>2.2. Consequential system delimitation </header>

Consequential modelling, applied in this study, is characterised by including the affected suppliers (sometimes referred to as marginal suppliers) and by avoiding co-product allocation by system expansion (Weidema, 2003; Ekvall and Weidema, 2004; Weidema and Ekvall, 2009). Consequential modelling strives to include relevant suppliers, e.g. the actual affected (or marginal) suppliers on markets, instead of the average suppliers on these markets. In the case of modelling the effects of manure treatment, the choice of consequential modelling also has the effect that for all the by-products of the pig manure system, the product substitu- tions are taken into account. The by-products of the pig manure system are: electricity, heat, and N and P fertiliser. The marginal suppliers of some important products in Denmark are given. Marginal suppliers for electricity production are identified as long- term yearly average marginal technologies with a combination of energy sources from coal, natural gas and wind with the ratio of

51%, 43% and 6%, respectively (Lund et al., 2010). The data of marginal electricity production relating to efficiencies of coal- and natural gas-based plants are described in Schmidt, (2007). For wind power plants, the data in the Ecoinvent database are applied. Marginal heat is composed of a weighted average of several different local supplies made up of small individual oil-fired boilers and a district heating system based on small combined heat and electricity plants as well as large central power plants based on gas, coal and wood (based on Schmidt, 2007). The average marginal heat suppliers are estimated based on the heat plan in Denmark (Ramboll Danmark A/S et al., 2008) for the period 2010 to 2020 as 40% of coal-based combined heat and power (CHP) plants, 29% of natural gas-based CHP plants, 12% of biomass-based CHP plants, 15% of biomass-fired boilers and 4% of natural gas-fired boilers. Due to data limitation, the energy efficiency of the different heat plants is based on a coal-fired CHP plant in Denmark (Schmidt, 2007). Marginal supplies of artificial fertilisers (nitrogen and phosphorus) are identified as modern technologies for ammonia nitrate and triple super phosphate (Schmidt, 2007).

14: Twelve scenarios are considered on the basis of European practices and illustrated in Fig. 2. A reference scenario (S1) is established, which applies the chosen reference systems explained formerly for treatment, storage and land application (TRE1, STO1 and LA1; Table 1). To assess changes at the treatment stage, four scenarios (S1, S7, S9, and S11) applying TRE 1 to 4 with the anaer- obic lagoon and broadcast spreading systems (STO1 and LA1, respectively) were chosen. For changes at the storage stage, eight scenarios (S1 and S6 to S12) were selected by applying the two storage systems (STO1 and STO2) to the four treatment systems (TRE 1 to 4) with the broadcast spreading system (LA1). Finally, changes at the land application stage are considered in five scenarios (S1 to S5) with no treatment and the anaerobic lagoon system applying five spreading systems (LA1 to LA5).

available, the international scientific publications are considered. The data, specifically, in terms of N, P, and C contents, are inven- toried with a mass balance approach in which all inputs and outputs are balanced.

For N flows, the total N content in manure is categorised into the emissions of NH3, N20, NO, N2 and the N uptake by harvested plants. For NO3 emissions at the land application stage, the values are varied with the same ranges among different application techniques (see Table 3). Furthermore, the data for the NO3 emis- sion is very limited and the applied approach has considered only a farm-gate balance without indicating specific sources such as organic or artificial fertilisers (Kyllingbk and Hansen, 2007). It is thus assumed that the NO3 emissions at the land application stage from pig manure and artificial fertiliser are the same and excluded from the calculations. For the N uptake by the plants, it is assumed that the N content left in processed manure is 100% equivalent to the content in artificial fertiliser (based on experimental results in Daudén and Quílez, 2004 and Luxhoi et al., 2004). Only uncer- tainties of NH3 emissions are presented and included in the sensitivity analysis to determine the robustness of selected storage and land application systems since this parameter highly influences the results.

For P balance, the total P input in pig manure is balanced to the available and unavailable P outputs for cultivated plant uptake compared with artificial fertiliser. Based on (Eghball et al., 2005), the P content in pig manure is 100% equivalent to mineral fertiliser except pig manure encompassing incineration (INC) and thermal gasification (GAS) processes. P availability for INC and GAS is determined as 20% of the content of artificial fertiliser (based on Moller et al., 2007 and Kuligowski et al., 2008).

For C balance, the main emission considered is only CH4 because CO2 is determined as biogenic and excluded from the calculations. Although the C content in organic fertiliser can be sequestered and determined as carbon saving in the long term, C sequestration for pig manure is insignificant possibly due to its easy degradability (Fellman et al., 2009; Triberti et al., 2008).

Other inventories for energy and material consumptions and transport are based on the existing database in SimaPro, such as Ecoinvent data v2.0 (Frischknecht et al., 2007). All the inputs in terms of materials and energy are considered except capital goods for separation and thermal pre-treatment processes due to a lack of information.

19: <header>2.5. Method for life cycle impact assessment (LCIA) </header>
LCIA is performed using the STEPWISE2006 method (Weidema, 2009; Weidema et al., 2007). The STEPWISE2006 method is chosen because it is based on several recent LCIA methods: EDIP2003

In addition the STEPWISE2006 method includes a new consistent weighting method based on the budget constraint approach (Weidema, 2009). This method includes both midpoint and end-point (damage) impacts and provides a monetarisation approach for impact assessment. At midpoint level, the data of emissions, materials and energy are classified and characterised into 17 impact categories. Afterwards, the characterised impacts are converted into single scores by weighting factors. To identify the most significant impact categories, three different weighting methods (STEPWISE2006 excluding biogenic CO2, EDIP2003, and IMPACT2002+ including biogenic CH4; see the compared impact equivalents in Schmidt and Thrane, 2009) are applied. Based on this, the significant impact categories are selected and assessed at the midpoint level. The presented LCIA does not include all the impact categories because of constraints on the length of the article.

22: In order to identify significant impact categories, the baseline scenario (S1) is assessed with three LCIA methods (STEPWISE2006, IMPACT2002+ including biogenic methane, and EDIP2003). The comparison is based on the percentage of single scores for each impact. On average, the most significant impact categories for the methods are global warming, respiratory inorganics and terrestrial eutrophication. In fact, aquatic eutrophication using the EDIP2003 method represents a large percentage of the single score but the sum of the positive value from the N emissions (88%) and the negative value from the avoided P emissions (-80%) results in low contribution in Fig. 3. Furthermore, the IMPACT2002+ method excludes aquatic eutrophication in the calculation of the single score and cannot be used to identify the significance of this impact category. Some impact categories represent very low percentage and thus are not noticeable in Fig. 3. For example, the mineral extraction contributes less than 0.1% of single scores. Nevertheless, none of the LCIA methods includes phosphorus as non-renewable resources. This limitation reduces the actual contribution of mineral extraction due to the avoided P fertiliser from pig manure management.
In the later assessment, the results will be presented in the four most important categories - global warming, respiratory inor- ganics, terrestrial eutrophication and aquatic eutrophication.

24: The characterised results of the changes at the treatment stage (S1, and 11) are presented in Fig. 4.

For global warming potentials (GWP), the anaerobic digestion- based scenario (S7) performs the highest reduction of greenhouse gas emissions compared with the reference scenario/S1 (147 kg CO2-eq.; Fig. 4a). The main contributor to the saved CO2 is avoided energy and heat production followed by fertiliser substitutions. Although, the incineration- and gasification-based scenarios (S9 and 11) have higher substituted energy production than the anaerobic system (S7), the methane emission during the storage stage from the liquid manure part separated prior to the combus- tion results in the lower CO2 savings. However, S9 and 11 are still able to save up to 119 kg CO2-eq. per ton of raw pig manure compared with S1 (Fig. 4a). The impact results for GWP show that both efficient energy recovery systems with controls of GHG emissions at every handling stage are crucial for effective GHG reduction strategies.

For respiratory inorganics potentials (RIP), the incineration- based scenario (S9) has the lowest impact (0.043 kg PM2.5-eq.; Fig. 4b). From the detailed inventory analysis, the most significant contributor to this impact is ammonia emissions followed by nitrogen oxides (NOx) emissions. Since ammonia can potentially form a secondary particulate matter such as ammonium nitrate and ammonium sulphate (Erisman and Schaap, 2004), it is the impor- tant parameter to control if the aim is to reduce RIP. S9 and S11 emit less ammonia than S1 and S7 because most of the N content is burnt during the combustion process. Although NOx and sulphur dioxide (SO2) emissions during the combustion process contribute to RIP, the effect on the impact is less significant compared with the ammonia emissions. However, the RIP from the anaerobic diges- tion-based scenario (S7) is higher than the value from the reference scenario (S1). This is because S7 and S1 have the same ammonia emission rates but there is an NOx emission from the biogas combustion process for S7 (see Table 3). All in all, to reduce RIP from the pig manure management, the main key is to control ammonia emission at different handling stages.

For aquatic eutrophication potentials (AEP), S9 and S11 have the lowest impacts (0.6 kg NO3-eq.; Fig. 4d) whereas S1 and S7 have the same higher results (0.9 kg NO3eq.; Fig. 4d). The main contributors to this impact are nitrate and phosphate emissions followed by ammonia emission. High nitrate loss during storage in the anaerobic lagoon (20%, see Table 3) results in the higher values of AEP for S1 and S7 than the values for S9 and S11 where less N content is left in the processed manure. However, the impacts are reduced by the avoided phosphate emission during the fertiliser production. This is because S7 and S1 have the same ammonia emission rates but there is an NOx emission from the biogas combustion process for S7 (see Table 3). All in all, to reduce RIP from the pig manure management, the main key is to control ammonia emission at different handling stages.

For respiratory inorganics potentials (RIP), the incineration- based scenario (S9) has the lowest impact (0.043 kg PM2.5-eq.; Fig. 4b). From the detailed inventory analysis, the most significant contributor to this impact is ammonia emissions followed by nitrogen oxides (NOx) emissions. Since ammonia can potentially form a secondary particulate matter such as ammonium nitrate and ammonium sulphate (Erisman and Schaap, 2004), it is the impor- tant parameter to control if the aim is to reduce RIP. S9 and S11 emit less ammonia than S1 and S7 because most of the N content is burnt during the combustion process. Although NOx and sulphur dioxide (SO2) emissions during the combustion process contribute to RIP, the effect on the impact is less significant compared with the ammonia emissions. However, the RIP from the anaerobic diges- tion-based scenario (S7) is higher than the value from the reference scenario (S1). This is because S7 and S1 have the same ammonia emission rates but there is an NOx emission from the biogas combustion process for S7 (see Table 3). All in all, to reduce RIP from the pig manure management, the main key is to control ammonia emission at different handling stages.

For aquatic eutrophication potentials (AEP), S9 and S11 have the lowest impacts (0.6 kg NO3-eq.; Fig. 4d) whereas S1 and S7 have the same higher results (0.9 kg NO3eq.; Fig. 4d). The main contributors to this impact are nitrate and phosphate emissions followed by ammonia emission. High nitrate loss during storage in the anaerobic lagoon (20%, see Table 3) results in the higher values of AEP for S1 and S7 than the values for S9 and S11 where less N content is left in the processed manure. However, the impacts are reduced by the avoided phosphate emission during the fertiliser production. In the case that the nitrate emission is eliminated, the reduced AEP from the avoided P fertiliser will be noticeable.

For respiratory inorganics potentials (RIP), the incineration- based scenario (S9) has the lowest impact (0.043 kg PM2.5-eq.; Fig. 4b). From the detailed inventory analysis, the most significant contributor to this impact is ammonia emissions followed by nitrogen oxides (NOx) emissions. Since ammonia can potentially form a secondary particulate matter such as ammonium nitrate and ammonium sulphate (Erisman and Schaap, 2004), it is the impor- tant parameter to control if the aim is to reduce RIP. S9 and S11 emit less ammonia than S1 and S7 because most of the N content is burnt during the combustion process. Although NOx and sulphur dioxide (SO2) emissions during the combustion process contribute to RIP, the effect on the impact is less significant compared with the ammonia emissions. This is because S7 and S1 have the same ammonia emission rates but there is an NOx emission from the biogas combustion process for S7 (see Table 3). All in all, to reduce RIP from the pig manure management, the main key is to control ammonia emission at different handling stages.

For respiratory inorganics potentials (RIP), the incineration- based scenario (S9) has the lowest impact (0.043 kg PM2.5-eq.; Fig. 4b). From the detailed inventory analysis, the most significant contributor to this impact is ammonia emissions followed by nitrogen oxides (NOx) emissions. Since ammonia can potentially form a secondary particulate matter such as ammonium nitrate and ammonium sulphate (Erisman and Schaap, 2004), it is the impor- tant parameter to control if the aim is to reduce RIP. S9 and S11 emit less ammonia than S1 and S7 because most of the N content is burnt during the combustion process. Although NOx and sulphur dioxide (SO2) emissions during the combustion process contribute to RIP, the effect on the impact is less significant compared with the ammonia emissions. However, the RIP from the anaerobic diges- tion-based scenario (S7) is higher than the value from the reference scenario (S1). This is because S7 and S1 have the same ammonia emission rates but there is an NOx emission from the biogas combustion process for S7 (see Table 3). All in all, to reduce RIP from the pig manure management, the main key is to control ammonia emission at different handling stages.

For aquatic eutrophication potentials (AEP), S9 and S11 have the lowest impacts (0.6 kg NO3-eq.; Fig. 4d) whereas S1 and S7 have the same higher results (0.9 kg NO3eq.; Fig. 4d). The main contributors to this impact are nitrate and phosphate emissions followed by ammonia emission. High nitrate loss during storage in the anaerobic lagoon (20%, see Table 3) results in the higher values of AEP for S1 and S7 than the values for S9 and S11 where less N content is left in the processed manure. However, the impacts are reduced by the avoided phosphate emission during the fertiliser production. In the case that the nitrate emission is eliminated, the reduced AEP from the avoided P fertiliser will be noticeable.

to this impact is ammonia emission. The reduction of this impact coincides with RIP due to the fact that ammonia emissions are the main influencing factor. Hence, the results from this part further elaborate on the needs to reduce ammonia emission.

For aquatic eutrophication potentials (AEP), S9 and S11 have the lowest impacts (0.6 kg NO3-eq.; Fig. 4d) whereas S1 and S7 have the same higher results (0.9 kg NO3eq.; Fig. 4d). The main contributors to this impact are nitrate and phosphate emissions followed by ammonia emission. High nitrate loss during storage in the anaerobic lagoon (20%, see Table 3) results in the higher values of AEP for S1 and S7 than the values for S9 and S11 where less N content is left in the processed manure. However, the impacts are reduced by the avoided phosphate emission during the fertiliser production. In the case that the nitrate emission is eliminated, the reduced AEP from the avoided P fertiliser will be noticeable.
13: In order to determine which stage most influences the envi- ronmental impacts, impact assessment results for S1, S7, S9 and S11 are separated into the four handling stages (Table 4).

For GWP, the most important stage for the scenarios with energy recovery is the treatment due to avoided heat and electricity production, whereas, for the no treatment scenario (S1), it is the storage due to methane emissions. Nevertheless, for anaerobic digestion-based scenario (S7), the land application stage is also important for GWP reduction due to the avoided N and P fertiliser. For RIP and TEP, the most significant stage is the land application followed by the storage due to the high amount of ammonia emissions during both stages for all scenarios. For AEP, the most significant stages are the storage and land application stages due to nitrate and avoided phosphate emissions, respectively. In addition,
at the transport stage, the different transport distances do not significantly affect the considered impacts.
14: The LCIA results of the eight integrated scenarios at the storage stage (S1 and S6 to S12) are shown in Fig. 4. When comparing scenarios applying the same treatment systems; for instance, S1 compared with S6, the scenarios using the storage tank with a natural crust (S6, S8, S10, and S12) can reduce GWP up to 94 kg CO2-eq. (Fig. 4a). Pig slurry stored in anaerobic lagoons (STO1, Table 1) is typically a significant source of methane, ammonia and nitrate losses resulting in less capacity for nutrient recovery through land application as can be seen from the emission factors in Table 3. The natural crust covering the storage tank (STO2, Table 1) can reduce the emissions significantly (57% for CH4, 66% for NH3, and 100% for NO; Table 3). Petersen et al. (2005) presented the direct evidence of methane oxidation in slurry storage with surface crusts resulting in lower methane emissions. The methane oxidation could occur in a porous surface crust with an access to O2 from the atmosphere. Negatively, from STO2, N20 can emit due to nitrification and denitrification activities from the coverage (Petersen and Miller, 2006). However, the N20 emission is insignificant when consid- ering the positive environmental impacts from lower methane emissions resulting in the GWP reduction of the natural crust storage scenarios. For RIP and TEP, they can be slightly decreased by an average of 12% when applying the same treatment systems (Fig. 4b and c). The impact reduction is derived from less ammonia volatilisation due to the crust coverage (Rotz, 2004). However, the N20 emission is insignificant when consid- ering the positive environmental impacts from lower methane emissions resulting in the GWP reduction of the natural crust storage scenarios. For RIP and TEP, they can be slightly decreased by an average of 12% when applying the same treatment systems (Fig. 4b and c). The impact reduction is derived from less ammonia volatilisation due to the crust coverage (Rotz, 2004). 19: <header>3.4. Changes at the land application stage </header>

The midpoint results of the five integrated scenarios at the land application stage (S1 to S5) are shown in Fig. 4. The scenarios with chosen alternative spreading systems (S2 to S4) can significantly reduce RIP and TEP from the reference scenario (S1) due to lower ammonia emissions (Fig. 4b and c) whereas GWP and AEP of the scenarios are similar (Fig. 4a and d). S5 applying deep injection system can most reduce RIP and TEP from S1 using broadcast spreading with an average of 67%. The spreading techniques can limit ammonia volatilisation through a reduced contact area between the manure and the ambient air, and a larger surface area for infiltration of the manure into the soil (Huijsmans et al., 2003). The reduction of ammonia emissions can also occur as a result of other climate conditions near the soil surface such as lower wind speeds and temperature (Thomson et al., 1990). The deep injection scenario (S5) can reduce more contact area between the manure and the ambient air than the other methods because the manure is completely covered by the soil (Rotz, 2004; Huijsmans et al., 2003). Furthermore, the infiltration of the manure can considerably reduce the influence from climate conditions. For AEP, the impact can be slightly decreased from the less ammonia emission because the most significant contributor to this impact is not the ammonia emissions but the nitrate and phosphate emissions. For GWP, all the LA systems result in similar impact values but the scenarios with the injection methods (S4 and S5) have slightly higher GWP from higher N20 emissions than the other methods. The N20 emission can occur

as a result of the denitrification process due to the higher available N content in the soil (Rotz, 2004). Nonetheless, in general, the GWP values are more or less the same among the different methods.

21: <header>3.5. Comparative changes at the treatment, storage and land application stages </header>

The twelve scenarios representing all the considered changes are compared with the reference scenario (S1) with respect to each significant impact category (Fig. 4). For GWP, the anaerobic diges- tion-based scenarios (S7 and S8) yield the highest reduction values (144-147 kg CO2-eq.; Fig. 4a) due to the high efficiencies of both energy and nutrient recovery with restricted GHG emissions. For RIP and TEP, the scenarios using the incineration and thermal gasification systems (S10 and S12) achieve the highest reduction values (RIP: 0.04 kg PM2.5-eq., TEP: 60 m² UES, approximately; Fig. 4b and c) due to the restricted ammonia emissions with less N content left in the pig manure. However, the scenario without a treatment system applying the deep injection method (S5) also yields the similar lowest RIP and TEP (Fig. 4b and c). For AEP, the scenarios with no treatment and anaerobic digestion systems applying the natural crust cover storage (S6 and S8) have the highest impact saving (1.9 kg NO3-eq.; Fig. 4d) because of limited nitrate loss and high P fertiliser substitution.

The findings imply that the main key for impact reduction is the effective treatment systems aiming at energy recovery with high available nutrients (N and P), and the control of ammonia, nitrate and GHG emissions at every handling stage.

23: The sensitivity analyses are carried out to determine the robustness of the results by considering how sensible parameters influence the LCIA results. Four major influencing parameters and their effects are analysed and described below.
24: Prapaspongsa et al. (2010) showed that the DM content in pig manure highly influenced on the environmental outcomes (net energy recovery and CO2 savings). The DM content of 8.3% measured at a Danish farm (Prapaspongsa et al., 2010) is thus selected to identify the sensitivity from high DM controlling. The nutrient content is calculated proportionally to the DM content. In Fig. 5, the sensitivity scenarios of the high DM content (S1A, S7A, S9A, and S11A) and the treatment scenarios in this study (S1, S7, S9, and S11) are compared. It shows that the higher the DM content in the pig manure is, the higher RIP, TEP and AEP occur (with a factor of two on average), when comparing the same treatment scenarios (e.g. S1 and S1A; Fig. 5b, C and d). The higher N content in pig manure induces higher ammonia and nitrate emissions resulting in the increased RIP, TEP and AEP. However, GWP can be saved increasingly with a factor of three on average for the higher DM scenarios with energy recovery (S7A, S9A, and S11A; Fig. 5a) because of the increased net energy production. In the case that the emissions of GHG, ammonia and nitrate are well controlled by using a proper storage and spreading systems such as the natural crust covering and deep injection, the benefits from increased DM content will be obvious.
25: <header>3.6.2. Uncertainties of ammonia emissions at the storage and land application stages </header>

Due to the fact that the emission factors of different storage and spreading systems depend on many factors such as weather conditions, soil conditions and manure characteristics, the uncer- tainties of ammonia emissions - the main influencing factor for RIP and TEP - are included in the life cycle impact assessment and presented as ranges of intervals in Figs. 4 and 5 (see uncertainty ranges of the emission factors in Table 3). The uncertainties of land

application systems are included in S1 to S12 in Fig. 4 whereas those of the storage systems are included in S1 and S6 to S12 in Fig. 5. The effect of the uncertainties both of land application and storage systems on GWP and AEP is insignificant because it does not change the ranks of the compared scenarios resulting in similar conclusions (Figs. 4a, d, 5a and d). For RIP and TEP, the uncertainties of land application systems show large intervals of values but do not generally influence the scenario ranking except the changes at the storage stage (Fig. 4b and c). The uncertainty ranges indicate that the two storage systems are not different. Furthermore, with respect to the uncertainties of the storage systems, the RIP and TEP with the uncertainty ranges present the similarity between the two storage systems when comparing the same treatment scenarios (e. g. S1 versus S6, and S7 versus S8; Fig. 5b and c). However, the similarity in RIP and TEP can be explained by the slightly less amount of ammonia emissions in the lower interval of the uncer- tainties of the storage systems because of the other loss of nitrogen content (nitrate) for the anaerobic lagoon system resulting in the higher AEP. Therefore, it implies the need to consider all the significant impacts to identify the best management options.

3.6.4. Marginal suppliers for electricity and heat substitution Prapaspongsa (2010) compared the business-as-us-usual marginal technologies (coal and natural gas-based technologies for elec- tricity and heat production, respectively) with the marginal tech- nologies identified in this study. The results presented that the scenario ranking was not changed but the GWP saving were increased with 41% on average for the scenarios applying the technologies aiming at energy extraction because the coal-based electricity production contributed to higher CO2 rate than the identified marginal electricity. Nevertheless, in case that the marginal suppliers are based on cleaner technologies such as wind or hydropower, the GWP saving and benefits from energy recovery will be smaller. Although the sensitivity factors affect the degrees of the impacts, they do not change the main keys for impact reduction, which are the effective energy recovery systems (anaerobic diges- tion, incineration and thermal gasification) and the control of GHG, ammonia and nitrate emissions at every handling stage.

33: This LCA study presents and compares twelve integrated tech- nological changes at the treatment, storage and land application stages, and also considers new significant impact categories in pig manure management. On the basis of the life cycle impact assess- ment (LCIA) results, it appears that the most important impact categories arising from pig manure management are global warming (GWP), aquatic eutrophication (AEP), respiratory inor- ganics (RIP, respiratory effects on human health from inorganic substances), and terrestrial eutrophication (TEP). The latter two are caused by ammonia emissions and have generally not been considered in LCA studies on pig manure management before. The importance of RIP and TEP suggest that there is a need for ammonia emission controls to reduce the overall environmental burdens from the piggery waste. The main keys for the effective impact minimisation from pig manure management are 1) integration of treatment technology systems aiming at energy recovery in combination with high nutrient recovery for GWP saving and 2) control of greenhouse gas, ammonia, and nitrate emission at every handling stage for GWP, RIP, TEP and AEP reduction. For GWP, the anaerobic digestion-based scenarios achieve the highest reduction values from the reference scenario (144-147 kg CO2-eq. per ton of raw pig manure) because of the high efficiencies in energy and nutrient recovery with restricted GHG emissions. For AEP, the anaerobic digestion- and no treatment-based scenarios with natural crust storage have the highest impact reduction (1.9 kg NO3-eq. per ton of raw pig manure) due to the high nutrient recovery and limited nitrate and ammonia emissions. With respect to the least ammonia emissions, for RIP and TEP, the incineration- and thermal gasification-based scenarios and the scenario without a treatment system applying the deep injection method yield the highest reduction values (RIP: 0.07-0.08 kg PM2.5-eq., and TEP: 80-86 m² UES per ton of raw pig manure). To assess the robustness of the findings, sensitivity analyses were performed. Uncertainties of ammonia emissions from the land application and storage systems are large. The sensitivity scenarios with a high DM content, with different typical storage and land application systems and different marginal electricity and heat production result in the different ranges of the considered environmental impacts. Never- theless, they do not change the main keys for the impact reduction in this investigation.

With respect to the integration of effective technologies and the newly considered environmental impacts, arising when the LCIA methods are applied, this study is useful for engineers, researchers and also decision makers to plan or research how to improve pig manure management systems at both national and global levels. In addition, it enables us to consider proper pig manure management as an abatement method for the particulate matter reduction. However, the results are based on emission factors published in selected liter- ature and they significantly depend on many factors such as weather conditions, soil conditions and manure characteristics. To apply this research effectively, it requires local data collection (e.g. marginal suppliers for by-products), inclusion of phosphorus as a non- renewable resource, and considerations of investment costs and capacities to implement the technologies in practice.
